title: The Delphi Murders — Two Girls, One 2-Second Audio Clip, Seven Years
description: On February 13, 2017, Abby Williams and Libby German were murdered on a hiking trail near Delphi, Indiana. Libby managed to record a 2-second video of the suspect on her Snapchat. Seven years later, Richard Allen was arrested. His trial revealed extraordinary claims of innocence, prosecutorial misconduct, and one of the most disturbing crime scenes in recent Indiana history.

segments.voice: February 13, 2017. Delphi, Indiana. A small town of about 3,000 people, surrounded by farmland and forests. Thirteen-year-old Abby Williams and 14-year-old Libby German are dropped off at the Monon High Bridge Trail for an afternoon hike. Libby takes photos and videos on her Snapchat as they walk across the old trestle bridge. They are supposed to be picked up a few hours later. They never come back.
segments.visual: Indiana rural trail bridge forest February winter

segments.voice: The next day, searchers found Abby and Libby's bodies in the woods near Deer Creek, not far from the trail. They had been murdered. The crime scene details were not fully disclosed to the public, but sources described the scene as unusually disturbing — indicating a prolonged attack and staging that investigators believed was deliberate and ritualistic.
segments.visual: search party forest Indiana creek woods discovery

segments.voice: But Libby German had done something extraordinary. As the man approached them on the bridge, she had activated her phone's camera and recorded him walking toward them. She then recorded a short audio clip in which a male voice can be heard saying two words: 'Down the hill.' This evidence — a grainy video of a man in a hat and jacket, and two seconds of audio — became the center of one of the most extraordinary public investigations in modern true crime.
segments.visual: phone camera recording bridge approaching figure trail

segments.voice: Indiana State Police released the video publicly, asking for the public's help identifying the suspect. The image showed a man in his 30s or 40s, wearing a blue jacket and a hat, walking with a distinctive gait. The audio of 'Down the hill' was analyzed by voice identification experts. Millions of people watched the clip. Tips flooded in — tens of thousands of them — from across the country.
segments.visual: surveillance blur image public release FBI tip line

segments.voice: For five years, the case remained unsolved despite one of the largest tip-line responses in Indiana law enforcement history. Investigators pursued thousands of leads. A second, clearer sketch of the suspect was released in 2019, which looked noticeably different from the original — suggesting police had developed new information. Still no arrest.

The community of Delphi was changed permanently. Parents became terrified to let children hike or play outdoors alone. The trail itself was closed. The families of Abby and Libby — particularly Libby's grandmother, Becky Patty, who had been one of the last people to see them — became vocal advocates for keeping the case in the public eye.
segments.visual: small town fear changed community trail closed

segments.voice: In October 2022 — five and a half years after the murders — Indiana State Police arrested Richard Allen, a 50-year-old pharmacist from Delphi. He was a local man. He had lived in Delphi. He had been interviewed by police in the original investigation and not flagged as a suspect. His arrest was announced with almost no advance information, stunning the community — including many who knew him.
segments.visual: arrest 2022 Richard Allen pharmacist Delphi Indiana

segments.voice: Evidence against Allen included unspent .40 caliber cartridges from a Sig Sauer pistol found at the crime scene. Police said they matched Allen's weapon. Statements Allen allegedly made to investigators were also cited. His phone was reportedly pinged in the area of the trail on the day of the murders. He was charged with two counts of murder.
segments.visual: ballistics evidence gun cartridge forensic match

segments.voice: What happened next was extraordinary and deeply troubling for the integrity of the case. Allen's defense team filed motions alleging that prosecutors had withheld evidence — specifically, that investigators had developed evidence pointing to members of a Norse pagan group allegedly active in the area, and that this evidence had been suppressed. The defense filed sensational, highly detailed claims. Investigators denied them.
segments.visual: legal defense motion suppressed evidence controversy court

segments.voice: Documents leaked from the case — in violation of a judicial gag order — spread across social media and true crime forums. The documents contained graphic details of the crime scene, allegations about the Norse pagan theory, and information about Allen's mental health deterioration in custody. Two defense attorneys were jailed for contempt. The case became a legal circus that threatened to derail the prosecution entirely.
segments.visual: leaked documents gag order contempt attorneys jailed

segments.voice: Allen's mental state in custody became a major issue. He was reportedly placed in a cell in deteriorating conditions, denied adequate mental health care, and allegedly coerced into making statements while in a delusional state. Defense attorneys argued these statements were not voluntary and could not be used as evidence. The judge in the case made multiple rulings excluding evidence.
segments.visual: mental health custody conditions prison deterioration

segments.voice: The original judge was removed from the case after a conflict of interest was alleged. A new judge, Fran Gull, took over. She made numerous rulings favorable to the prosecution. Defense attorneys continued to argue misconduct. The case drew national attention not just because of the original murders, but because the legal proceedings had become a demonstration of nearly every possible way a criminal case can go wrong.
segments.visual: judge removed replaced legal proceedings complex controversy

segments.voice: Richard Allen's trial began in October 2024. The prosecution presented their ballistic evidence, phone location data, and statements Allen had allegedly made. The defense argued the Norse pagan theory and challenged the evidence's reliability. Allen maintained his innocence throughout. After deliberating, the jury found Allen guilty of both murders. He was sentenced to two consecutive 65-year sentences.

The defense immediately filed appeals. Multiple legal analysts argued the case had been tainted — not by Allen's guilt or innocence, but by the procedural and investigative misconduct that had occurred. Whether the conviction will survive appeals is uncertain. Whatever happens in court, the community of Delphi is left with a verdict, a condemned man, and lingering questions about whether everything that could have been done to ensure a fair trial was done.
segments.visual: appeals legal challenge conviction integrity questions

segments.voice: Libby German's final act — recording the man on that bridge — remains one of the most significant pieces of inadvertent crime scene documentation ever captured. A 14-year-old girl, sensing danger, did the right thing. The evidence she created drove a seven-year investigation and ultimately led to a conviction. Whatever happens on appeal, her quick thinking gave investigators something to work with.
segments.visual: Libby German phone bridge recording tribute courage

segments.voice: Abby Williams was 13 years old. Libby German was 14. They went for a hike on a February afternoon and did not come home. Their families have spent seven years seeking justice — watching a case unfold with extraordinary complexity, controversy, and pain. Whatever the appellate courts ultimately decide, Abby and Libby deserve to be the center of this story. Not the legal proceduralism. Not the Norse pagan theories. Two children were killed. That is what matters.
segments.visual: memorial Abby Libby tribute Indiana community girls
